nativeRange.habitat: Deepwater coral reefs, seamounts, and offshore banks; recorded depth range generally 64–250 m (with reports to ~250 m / ~820 ft in Tahiti)

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on small fishes and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts thawed marine meats such as silversides, krill (preferably soaked in a vitamin supplement such as Selcon), squid, prawn, clam, mussel, and chopped marine fish. Large sinking pellets have been used as supplements. Feeder fish (especially freshwater feeders such as goldfish) are best avoided as a primary food. Infrequent, larger meals tend to be advised over frequent small feedings.

goodWith: robust predatory fish of comparable size
avoidWith: anything that fits in its mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by John E. Randall, 640x403. Only CC live-color photograph of Cephalopholis igarashiensis available; vivid gold body with pale vertical bars and diagnostic black eyespot on dorsal soft rays. Correctly ID'd, in focus. Caveat: low resolution and subadult/juvenile color phase rather than adult purple-and-gold.

summary: An extremely rare deepwater grouper from the western Pacific, prized for its vivid purple-and-gold coloration. Widely considered an aggressive ambush predator, it will readily consume any fish or crustacean small enough to fit in its mouth.
careNotes:
The Neptune Grouper (Cephalopholis igarashiensis) is among the rarest and most expensive groupers available to hobbyists, collected from deepwater reefs at roughly 64–250 m depth across the western Pacific — southern Japan, the Philippines, Taiwan, Guam, Fiji, Samoa, and French Polynesia. Care is commonly rated as advanced, reflecting the species' deepwater origins, large adult size (typically 25 cm, with a maximum around 43 cm), high waste output, and the level of attention required to meet its feeding and water-quality needs; it is not considered particularly fragile once it has settled in.

A tank of at least 1000 L (roughly 265 gal) is generally recommended for long-term adult housing, accounting for the species' size, territorial nature, and the volume of waste a large predatory fish produces.

The Neptune Grouper comes from dim, deep-reef environments and has been reported to be stressed by the intense lighting typical of modern reef aquariums. Subdued lighting and plenty of shaded rockwork — caves, overhangs, and deep crevices — tend to be recommended so the fish can behave naturally. It is generally described as an ambush predator that may claim a single cave as a territory and defend it, while remaining relatively reserved in open water otherwise. The species poses minimal direct threat to corals but will readily consume ornamental shrimp, small fish, and other small invertebrates, so it is generally not considered reef-safe in the traditional sense, even though it poses no direct threat to corals themselves.

Juveniles display markedly different coloration from adults, including a prominent black eyespot on the soft-rayed portion of the dorsal fin. Captive breeding has not been documented.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Cephalopholis igarashiensis has not been documented in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry recommendations: tank minimum (180 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), temperament (aggressive), reef-safe (with caution), and diet.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Epinephelidae / Perciformes), maximum size (43 cm TL, common 25 cm), depth range (80–250 m), wild temperature range (18.3–26.5°C, mean 21.3°C), and native range.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, maximum standard length (43 cm), depth range (64–250 m), western-Pacific distribution, and the juvenile-vs-adult color phase (juveniles show a black eyespot on the dorsal soft rays).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (180 gal min, 'bigger is better'), confirmed deepwater origin and reported sensitivity to bright reef-aquarium lighting, and described territorial cave-claiming behaviour.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25–43 cm), depth (80–250 m), diet (fishes, crustaceans, mysis), aggressive temperament, and the much-larger tank recommendation of at least 3000 L — used to bias the tank-size pick upward.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby. Most Cephalopholis are protogynous hermaphrodites, but this has not been directly confirmed for C. igarashiensis.
